• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Case Study 2: Factor Analysis – Understanding Quality Perceptions of Smartphones

Giovanni Carta, Zahra Ghafghazi, Sophia Ropelewski

2026-04-14

1 - Data Preparation

As a first step, we start by checking the size of the dataset, and by getting the variables of interest. The dataset contains responses from 979 US smartphone customers who rated their device on 33 quality perception items (qd1-qd33) using 5-point scales.

# import the data
data <- read.csv("Data File_Case_Study_Factor Analysis_MD.csv")

# only select the quality perception items of interest
quality_data <- data %>%
  select(all_of(paste0("qd", 1:33)))

# check the size
cat("Sample size:", nrow(data), "\n")
## Sample size: 978
cat("Quality items:", ncol(quality_data), "\n")
## Quality items: 33

1.1 - Missing Data

Next, we need to check for missing data and handle them in an appropriate way.

# Count NAs per item and reshape to long format
missing_summary <- quality_data %>%
  # single-row data frame where each column contains its count of NA
  summarise(across(everything(), ~ sum(is.na(.)))) %>% 
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Item", values_to = "N_Missing") %>% # pivot the dataframe
  filter(N_Missing > 0) %>% # only keep rows containing NA
  mutate(Percentage = round(N_Missing / nrow(quality_data) * 100, 1))  # get the percentage

missing_summary %>%
  kbl(caption = "Items with missing values") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Items with missing values
Item N_Missing Percentage
qd22 45 4.6
qd26 45 4.6
qd28 45 4.6
qd29 45 4.6
qd31 45 4.6
qd33 45 4.6

Six items (qd22, qd26, qd28, qd29, qd31, qd33) each have 45 missing values. Below, we verify if the same 45 respondents are always missing across all six items.

# verify if the NAs belong to the same rows
n_block <- sum(is.na(quality_data$qd22) & is.na(quality_data$qd26) &
               is.na(quality_data$qd28) & is.na(quality_data$qd29) &
               is.na(quality_data$qd31) & is.na(quality_data$qd33))
cat("Respondents missing on all six items:", n_block, "\n")
## Respondents missing on all six items: 45

All 45 missing cases come from the same respondents. Since the affected cases represent less than 5% of the sample, we decide to remove the 45 rows containing a missing value.

# drop the rows containing NA
quality_data_complete <- quality_data %>% drop_na()
cat("Complete-case n:", nrow(quality_data_complete), "\n")
## Complete-case n: 933
cat("Remaining missing values:", sum(is.na(quality_data_complete)), "\n")
## Remaining missing values: 0

2 - Exercise 1: Orthogonal Factor Analysis (Varimax)

2.1 - Factorability Assessment

2.1.1 - Correlation Matrix

Now that we have cleaned our data, we first need to assess if factor analysis can be performed on it. For this, we calculate the correlation matrix below.

# Correlation matrix calculation
cor_mat <- cor(quality_data_complete)

corrplot(cor_mat, method = "color", type = "upper", tl.cex = 0.62, tl.col = "black",
         col = colorRampPalette(c("#c0392b", "white", "#2980b9"))(200),
         title = "Correlation Matrix - Quality Items (qd1-qd33)", mar = c(0,0,2,0))

# Get the top 20 highest correlations between items
cor_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat)) %>%
  setNames(c("Item_1", "Item_2", "r")) %>%
  filter(as.character(Item_1) < as.character(Item_2)) %>%
  mutate(abs_r = abs(r)) %>%
  arrange(desc(abs_r))

head(cor_long, 20) %>%
  kbl(digits = 3, caption = "Top 20 pairwise correlations") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Top 20 pairwise correlations
Item_1 Item_2 r abs_r
qd10 qd19 0.879 0.879
qd14 qd24 0.875 0.875
qd15 qd18 0.857 0.857
qd10 qd24 0.854 0.854
qd19 qd24 0.844 0.844
qd22 qd29 0.843 0.843
qd14 qd19 0.842 0.842
qd21 qd24 0.841 0.841
qd2 qd9 0.840 0.840
qd18 qd32 0.840 0.840
qd2 qd20 0.839 0.839
qd15 qd32 0.838 0.838
qd20 qd27 0.836 0.836
qd2 qd27 0.835 0.835
qd20 qd9 0.831 0.831
qd10 qd14 0.830 0.830
qd27 qd9 0.830 0.830
qd13 qd30 0.824 0.824
qd1 qd7 0.820 0.820
qd14 qd21 0.819 0.819

The correlation matrix shows a pattern of moderate to high positive correlations between the items (shown in blue), with the values of the top 20 highest correlations values situated between 0.879 and 0.819. This suggests the existence of common latent factors.

2.1.2 - KMO and Bartlett’s Test

Before running factor analysis, we also calculate the KMO (Kaiser-Meyer-Olkin), which checks if variables share enough common variance, and perform the Bartlett’s test, to see if the correlation matrix is an identity matrix or not.

# calculate overall KMO and Bartlett's test
kmo_result      <- KMO(cor_mat)
bartlett_result <- cortest.bartlett(cor_mat, n = nrow(quality_data_complete))

cat("Overall KMO:", round(kmo_result$MSA, 3), "\n")
## Overall KMO: 0.962
cat("Bartlett chi-square:", round(bartlett_result$chisq, 2),
    "| df:", bartlett_result$df,
    "| p-value:", bartlett_result$p.value, "\n")
## Bartlett chi-square: 29710.79 | df: 528 | p-value: 0

The overall KMO of 0.96 indicates excellent sampling adequacy. Bartlett’s test is highly significant (p < .001), confirming the correlation matrix is sufficiently different from an identity matrix. Both criteria support proceeding with factor analysis.

2.1.3 - Anti-Image Correlation Matrix

In addition to the overall KMO result and the Bartlett’s test, we can also have a look at the anti-image correlation matrix, which provides item-level evidence of factorability. The diagonal elements are the individual KMO (MSA) values for each item. Off-diagonal elements should be small in magnitude. This indicates that partial correlations between items are negligible once shared factor variance is removed.

# print items with lowest MSA  using the previous calculate kmo
msa_df <- data.frame(
  Item = names(kmo_result$MSAi),
  MSA  = round(kmo_result$MSAi, 3)
) %>%
  mutate(
    Rating = case_when(
      MSA >= 0.90 ~ "Excellent",
      MSA >= 0.80 ~ "Good",
      MSA >= 0.70 ~ "Acceptable",
      MSA >= 0.60 ~ "Mediocre",
      TRUE        ~ "Unacceptable"
    )
  ) %>%
  arrange(MSA)  # lowest MSA first

msa_df %>%
  kbl(caption = "Item-level KMO (MSA) (lowest first)",
      col.names = c("Item", "MSA", "Rating")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  column_spec(2, bold = TRUE,
              color = ifelse(msa_df$MSA >= 0.70, "#27ae60", "#c0392b"))
Item-level KMO (MSA) (lowest first)
Item MSA Rating
qd4 qd4 0.654 Mediocre
qd23 qd23 0.905 Excellent
qd22 qd22 0.929 Excellent
qd29 qd29 0.935 Excellent
qd15 qd15 0.947 Excellent
qd18 qd18 0.952 Excellent
qd33 qd33 0.952 Excellent
qd11 qd11 0.954 Excellent
qd3 qd3 0.957 Excellent
qd6 qd6 0.957 Excellent
qd24 qd24 0.957 Excellent
qd25 qd25 0.957 Excellent
qd10 qd10 0.959 Excellent
qd19 qd19 0.959 Excellent
qd31 qd31 0.959 Excellent
qd14 qd14 0.960 Excellent
qd13 qd13 0.961 Excellent
qd28 qd28 0.962 Excellent
qd2 qd2 0.963 Excellent
qd30 qd30 0.964 Excellent
qd32 qd32 0.964 Excellent
qd1 qd1 0.966 Excellent
qd20 qd20 0.966 Excellent
qd27 qd27 0.966 Excellent
qd26 qd26 0.967 Excellent
qd12 qd12 0.969 Excellent
qd7 qd7 0.970 Excellent
qd9 qd9 0.970 Excellent
qd17 qd17 0.970 Excellent
qd21 qd21 0.974 Excellent
qd5 qd5 0.979 Excellent
qd8 qd8 0.982 Excellent
qd16 qd16 0.982 Excellent 
# heatmap of the anti-image correlation matrix (diagonal suppressed for clarity)
# low values are preferable
anti_image_plot <- kmo_result$ImC
diag(anti_image_plot) <- NA

corrplot(anti_image_plot,
         method       = "color",
         type         = "upper",
         tl.cex       = 0.62,
         tl.col       = "black",
         na.label     = "square",
         na.label.col = "#bdc3c7",
         col          = colorRampPalette(c("#c0392b", "white", "#2980b9"))(200),
         title        = "Anti-Image Correlation Matrix (diagonal suppressed)",
         mar          = c(0, 0, 2, 0))

The majority of off-diagonal anti-image correlations are close to zero, confirming that partial correlations are negligible and items share enough common variance. The single item with the lowest MSA is qd4 (0.65, mediocre), which we should monitor.

2.2 - Number of Factors Assessment

2.2.1 - Initial Assessment

We have determined that the data is suitable for factor analysis. The next step is to decide on the number of factors we need. Three criteria will be used to determine this number: The Kaiser rule (eigenvalues greater than 1), a scree plot, and parallel analysis.

# Calculation of the eigenvalues
eigen_values <- eigen(cor_mat)$values

# create a datable with the 12 highest eigenvalues, their value and cumulative variance
data.frame(Factor = 1:12,
           Eigenvalue = round(eigen_values[1:12], 3),
           Cum_Percentage = round(cumsum(eigen_values[1:12]) / sum(eigen_values) * 100, 1)) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Eigenvalues (first 12 factors)") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(which(eigen_values[1:12] >= 1), bold = TRUE, background = "#ebf5fb")
Eigenvalues (first 12 factors)
Factor Eigenvalue Cum_Percentage
1 15.938 48.3
2 2.335 55.4
3 2.042 61.6
4 1.498 66.1
5 1.389 70.3
6 1.182 73.9
7 1.089 77.2
8 1.009 80.2
9 0.851 82.8
10 0.759 85.1
11 0.405 86.4
12 0.363 87.5

We first check the eigenvalue bar chart below, which shows the 12 factors with the highest eigenvalues and percentage of variance explained. The bar chart tells us that 8 factors have an eigenvalue above 1.

# screeplot
plot(eigen_values[1:15], type = "b", pch = 19, col = "#2980b9",
     main = "Scree Plot", xlab = "Factor", ylab = "Eigenvalue")
abline(h = 1, col = "#c0392b", lty = 2)

With the screeplot alone, it is difficult to determine where the “elbow” is. Using the Kaiser criterion (eigenvalue above 1, red dotted line), we see where the limit would be.

# run parallel analysis
fa.parallel(quality_data_complete, fa = "fa", fm = "pa",
            main = "Parallel Analysis - Factor Retention")

## Parallel analysis suggests that the number of factors =  8  and the number of components =  NA

The Kaiser rule retains 8 factors, the scree plot elbow falls between 7 and 8 and parallel analysis suggests 8. From the above results, we conclude that the 8-factor solution seem to be the best starting point. But to make the analysis complete, we will also calculate and compare 7 and 9-factor solutions in the following sections.

2.2.2 - Comparison of possible solutions

As stated previously, we try out 3 different solutions : 7, 8 and 9-factor solutions using \(varimax\) method (orthogonal factor analysis).

fa_7 <- fa(quality_data_complete, nfactors = 7, fm = "pa", rotate = "varimax")
fa_8 <- fa(quality_data_complete, nfactors = 8, fm = "pa", rotate = "varimax")
fa_9 <- fa(quality_data_complete, nfactors = 9, fm = "pa", rotate = "varimax")

cat("=== 7-Factor Solution ===\n"); print(fa_7, cut = 0.30, sort = TRUE)
## === 7-Factor Solution ===
## Factor Analysis using method =  pa
## Call: fa(r = quality_data_complete, nfactors = 7, rotate = "varimax", 
##     fm = "pa")
## Standardized loadings (pattern matrix) based upon correlation matrix
##      item  PA2   PA1  PA5   PA3   PA6  PA7   PA4    h2   u2 com
## qd24   24 0.86                                   0.882 0.12 1.4
## qd14   14 0.85                                   0.850 0.15 1.4
## qd10   10 0.83                                   0.845 0.16 1.5
## qd19   19 0.83                                   0.842 0.16 1.5
## qd21   21 0.79                                   0.776 0.22 1.5
## qd7     7       0.74                             0.833 0.17 2.2
## qd1     1       0.73                             0.808 0.19 2.2
## qd12   12       0.71                             0.778 0.22 2.3
## qd5     5       0.64                  0.31       0.718 0.28 2.8
## qd16   16       0.61                             0.637 0.36 2.6
## qd2     2            0.78                        0.843 0.16 1.8
## qd20   20            0.76                        0.833 0.17 2.0
## qd9     9            0.74                        0.836 0.16 2.2
## qd27   27            0.74                        0.837 0.16 2.2
## qd22   22                  0.84                  0.837 0.16 1.4
## qd29   29                  0.81                  0.806 0.19 1.5
## qd33   33                  0.78                  0.741 0.26 1.5
## qd4     4                                        0.031 0.97 2.8
## qd15   15                        0.77            0.826 0.17 1.9
## qd18   18                        0.75            0.812 0.19 2.1
## qd32   32                        0.73            0.798 0.20 2.2
## qd31   31                  0.38  0.50            0.536 0.46 3.3
## qd26   26                  0.39  0.41            0.509 0.49 4.1
## qd28   28       0.33       0.35  0.40            0.481 0.52 4.2
## qd25   25                             0.75       0.779 0.22 1.8
## qd6     6                             0.74       0.823 0.18 2.1
## qd17   17                             0.63       0.644 0.36 2.4
## qd8     8       0.35                  0.56       0.676 0.32 3.7
## qd23   23                                        0.084 0.92 3.2
## qd3     3                                   0.76 0.775 0.22 1.8
## qd11   11                                   0.74 0.711 0.29 1.7
## qd13   13                                   0.73 0.800 0.20 2.2
## qd30   30                                   0.67 0.771 0.23 2.8
## 
##                        PA2  PA1  PA5  PA3  PA6  PA7  PA4
## SS loadings           4.61 3.66 3.34 3.14 3.08 2.97 2.95
## Proportion Var        0.14 0.11 0.10 0.10 0.09 0.09 0.09
## Cumulative Var        0.14 0.25 0.35 0.45 0.54 0.63 0.72
## Proportion Explained  0.19 0.15 0.14 0.13 0.13 0.13 0.12
## Cumulative Proportion 0.19 0.35 0.49 0.62 0.75 0.88 1.00
## 
## Mean item complexity =  2.2
## Test of the hypothesis that 7 factors are sufficient.
## 
## df null model =  528  with the objective function =  32.29 with Chi Square =  29710.79
## df of  the model are 318  and the objective function was  1.37 
## 
## The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.02 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.03 
## 
## The harmonic n.obs is  933 with the empirical chi square  428.93  with prob <  3.2e-05 
## The total n.obs was  933  with Likelihood Chi Square =  1251.01  with prob <  1e-110 
## 
## Tucker Lewis Index of factoring reliability =  0.947
## RMSEA index =  0.056  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.053 0.059
## BIC =  -923.6
## Fit based upon off diagonal values = 1
## Measures of factor score adequacy             
##                                                    PA2  PA1  PA5  PA3  PA6  PA7
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.97 0.92 0.94 0.94 0.93 0.91
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.94 0.85 0.89 0.89 0.86 0.82
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.88 0.71 0.78 0.77 0.71 0.65
##                                                    PA4
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.93
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.86
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.72
cat("\n=== 8-Factor Solution ===\n"); print(fa_8, cut = 0.30, sort = TRUE)
## 
## === 8-Factor Solution ===
## Factor Analysis using method =  pa
## Call: fa(r = quality_data_complete, nfactors = 8, rotate = "varimax", 
##     fm = "pa")

## qd20   20           0.76                             0.834 0.17 2.0

## qd8     8      0.34            0.56                  0.678 0.32 3.8
## qd23   23                                            0.088 0.91 3.9
## qd22   22                            0.82            0.853 0.15 1.6
## qd29   29                            0.80            0.830 0.17 1.6

## qd15   15                                 0.77       0.864 0.14 2.1
## qd18   18                                 0.74       0.849 0.15 2.3

## qd28   28                                       0.66 0.681 0.32 2.3

## qd4     4                                            0.043 0.96 2.4
## 
##                        PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA7  PA3  PA6  PA8
## SS loadings           4.64 3.63 3.37 2.94 2.88 2.66 2.45 1.91

## Cumulative Proportion 0.19 0.34 0.48 0.60 0.71 0.82 0.92 1.00

## Test of the hypothesis that 8 factors are sufficient.
## 
## df null model =  528  with the objective function =  32.29 with Chi Square =  29710.79
## df of  the model are 292  and the objective function was  0.72 
## 
## The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.01 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.02 
## 
## The harmonic n.obs is  933 with the empirical chi square  145.76  with prob <  1 
## The total n.obs was  933  with Likelihood Chi Square =  661.63  with prob <  1e-30 
## 
## Tucker Lewis Index of factoring reliability =  0.977
## RMSEA index =  0.037  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.033 0.041
## BIC =  -1335.19
## Fit based upon off diagonal values = 1
## Measures of factor score adequacy

## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.94 0.85 0.90 0.86 0.83 0.88
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.88 0.71 0.79 0.72 0.66 0.76
##                                                    PA6  PA8
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.93 0.85
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.87 0.72
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.75 0.45
cat("\n=== 9-Factor Solution ===\n"); print(fa_9, cut = 0.30, sort = TRUE)
## 
## === 9-Factor Solution ===
## Factor Analysis using method =  pa
## Call: fa(r = quality_data_complete, nfactors = 9, rotate = "varimax", 
##     fm = "pa")

##      item  PA2   PA1  PA5   PA4   PA3   PA7   PA6   PA8   PA9   h2   u2 com

## qd15   15                                    0.75             0.86 0.14 2.2
## qd18   18                                    0.74             0.86 0.14 2.3
## qd32   32                                    0.71             0.83 0.17 2.5
## qd28   28                                          0.66       0.68 0.32 2.3
## qd31   31                                          0.65       0.70 0.30 2.5
## qd26   26                                          0.61       0.65 0.35 2.7

## 
##                        PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA3  PA7  PA6  PA8  PA9
## SS loadings           4.64 3.70 3.39 3.16 2.76 2.51 2.34 1.88 0.51
## Proportion Var        0.14 0.11 0.10 0.10 0.08 0.08 0.07 0.06 0.02
## Cumulative Var        0.14 0.25 0.36 0.45 0.53 0.61 0.68 0.74 0.75
## Proportion Explained  0.19 0.15 0.14 0.13 0.11 0.10 0.09 0.08 0.02
## Cumulative Proportion 0.19 0.33 0.47 0.60 0.71 0.81 0.90 0.98 1.00
## 
## Mean item complexity =  2.1
## Test of the hypothesis that 9 factors are sufficient.
## 
## df null model =  528  with the objective function =  32.29 with Chi Square =  29710.79
## df of  the model are 267  and the objective function was  0.62 
## 
## The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.01 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.01 
## 
## The harmonic n.obs is  933 with the empirical chi square  59.82  with prob <  1 
## The total n.obs was  933  with Likelihood Chi Square =  569.28  with prob <  5.4e-24 
## 
## Tucker Lewis Index of factoring reliability =  0.979
## RMSEA index =  0.035  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.031 0.039
## BIC =  -1256.57
## Fit based upon off diagonal values = 1
## Measures of factor score adequacy             
##                                                    PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA3  PA7
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.97 0.93 0.95 0.93 0.94 0.91
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.94 0.86 0.89 0.86 0.88 0.83
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.88 0.71 0.79 0.72 0.77 0.65
##                                                    PA6  PA8   PA9
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.93 0.85  0.64
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.87 0.72  0.42
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.73 0.44 -0.17

2.2.3 - Fit Comparison

We then compare the 3 fits against each other.

# fit comparison
data.frame(
  Solution = c("7 Factors", "8 Factors", "9 Factors"),
  RMSEA    = round(c(fa_7$RMSEA[1], fa_8$RMSEA[1], fa_9$RMSEA[1]), 3),
  TLI      = round(c(fa_7$TLI,      fa_8$TLI,      fa_9$TLI),      3),
  BIC      = round(c(fa_7$BIC,      fa_8$BIC,      fa_9$BIC),      1),
  Var_Pct  = round(c(sum(fa_7$Vaccounted[2,]),
                     sum(fa_8$Vaccounted[2,]),
                     sum(fa_9$Vaccounted[2,])) * 100, 1)
) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Fit indices: 7-, 8-, and 9-factor solutions",
      col.names = c("Solution", "RMSEA", "TLI", "BIC", "Variance Explained (%)")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover"), full_width = FALSE) %>%
  row_spec(2, bold = TRUE, background = "#eafaf1")
Fit indices: 7-, 8-, and 9-factor solutions
Solution RMSEA TLI BIC Variance Explained (%)
7 Factors 0.056 0.947 -923.6 72.0
8 Factors 0.037 0.977 -1335.2 74.2
9 Factors 0.035 0.979 -1256.6 75.4

The 7-factor solution merges two factors into one factor, causing cross-loadings and reducing interpretability. The 9-factor solution only has a marginal fit improvement, but the ninth factor is defined almost entirely by qd4 and qd23. In addition, their loadings are not very high (approx. equal to 0.4), and we saw previously that qd4 is an item to be monitored. This suggests overfactoring. The 8-factor solution provides the best balance of fit, parsimony, and interpretability, with good loading values (above 0.6 for most of them). Only the items qd23 and qd4 have no important loading value, and qd8 has a loading at the limit of 0.6 (equal to 0.56) for one factor, and a cross-loading value of 0.34 for another factor. For all those reason, we will continue with the 8-factor analysis in the next parts of this analysis.

2.2.4 - Item Refinement

In the previous section, we have determined the number of factors. It is now time to refine the solution by trying to remove some items.

We have previously identified items qd4 and qd23 which do not have important loading values with the 8-factor solution. We therefore try to remove them and recalculate the factor analysis. We also observed that qd8 has an non-negligible cross-loading, and therefore try an additional solution in which this item is also removed. Finally, qd31 also has a cross-loading (but its loading is slightly more important, >0.6). We try a final solution without this item too.

# Scenario 1: Remove qd4 and qd23
quality_data_r1 <- quality_data_complete %>% select(-qd4, -qd23)
fa_r1 <- fa(quality_data_r1, nfactors = 8, fm = "pa", rotate = "varimax")
print(fa_r1, cut = 0.30, sort = TRUE)
## Factor Analysis using method =  pa
## Call: fa(r = quality_data_r1, nfactors = 8, rotate = "varimax", fm = "pa")
## Standardized loadings (pattern matrix) based upon correlation matrix
##      item  PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA3  PA7  PA6  PA8   h2   u2 com
## qd24   22 0.86                                    0.88 0.12 1.4
## qd14   13 0.85                                    0.85 0.15 1.4
## qd10    9 0.83                                    0.85 0.15 1.5
## qd19   18 0.83                                    0.84 0.16 1.5
## qd21   20 0.79                                    0.78 0.22 1.5
## qd7     6      0.74                               0.84 0.16 2.2
## qd1     1      0.72                               0.81 0.19 2.3
## qd12   11      0.72                               0.79 0.21 2.3
## qd5     4      0.64                               0.72 0.28 2.8
## qd16   15      0.62                               0.64 0.36 2.6
## qd2     2           0.79                          0.85 0.15 1.8
## qd20   19           0.77                          0.83 0.17 1.9
## qd9     8           0.75                          0.83 0.17 2.1
## qd27   25           0.75                          0.84 0.16 2.2
## qd3     3                0.77                     0.77 0.23 1.7
## qd11   10                0.75                     0.71 0.29 1.6
## qd13   12                0.74                     0.80 0.20 2.1
## qd30   28                0.68                     0.77 0.23 2.6
## qd22   21                     0.83                0.86 0.14 1.5
## qd29   27                     0.81                0.83 0.17 1.6
## qd33   31                     0.77                0.75 0.25 1.6
## qd25   23                          0.75           0.79 0.21 1.9
## qd6     5                          0.73           0.84 0.16 2.3
## qd17   16                          0.59           0.62 0.38 2.8
## qd8     7      0.35                0.55           0.68 0.32 4.0
## qd15   14                               0.75      0.86 0.14 2.2
## qd18   17                               0.74      0.86 0.14 2.3
## qd32   30                               0.72      0.83 0.17 2.5
## qd28   26                                    0.67 0.69 0.31 2.2
## qd31   29                                    0.66 0.70 0.30 2.4
## qd26   24                                    0.62 0.65 0.35 2.7
## 
##                        PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA3  PA7  PA6  PA8
## SS loadings           4.58 3.60 3.39 3.15 2.76 2.61 2.34 1.93
## Proportion Var        0.15 0.12 0.11 0.10 0.09 0.08 0.08 0.06
## Cumulative Var        0.15 0.26 0.37 0.47 0.56 0.65 0.72 0.79
## Proportion Explained  0.19 0.15 0.14 0.13 0.11 0.11 0.10 0.08
## Cumulative Proportion 0.19 0.34 0.47 0.60 0.72 0.82 0.92 1.00
## 
## Mean item complexity =  2.1
## Test of the hypothesis that 8 factors are sufficient.
## 
## df null model =  465  with the objective function =  32.07 with Chi Square =  29530.97
## df of  the model are 245  and the objective function was  0.6 
## 
## The root mean square of the residuals (RMSR) is  0.01 
## The df corrected root mean square of the residuals is  0.01 
## 
## The harmonic n.obs is  933 with the empirical chi square  53.31  with prob <  1 
## The total n.obs was  933  with Likelihood Chi Square =  546.41  with prob <  4.8e-25 
## 
## Tucker Lewis Index of factoring reliability =  0.98
## RMSEA index =  0.036  and the 90 % confidence intervals are  0.032 0.04
## BIC =  -1129
## Fit based upon off diagonal values = 1
## Measures of factor score adequacy             
##                                                    PA2  PA1  PA5  PA4  PA3  PA7
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.97 0.92 0.95 0.93 0.94 0.91
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.94 0.85 0.90 0.86 0.89 0.83
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.88 0.71 0.79 0.72 0.78 0.66
##                                                    PA6  PA8
## Correlation of (regression) scores with factors   0.93 0.85
## Multiple R square of scores with factors          0.87 0.72
## Minimum correlation of possible factor scores     0.74 0.45
# Scenario 2: Additionally remove qd8 
quality_data_final_ortho <- quality_data_complete %>% select(-qd4, -qd23, -qd8)
fa_final_ortho <- fa(quality_data_final_ortho, nfactors = 8, fm = "pa", rotate = "varimax")
print(fa_final_ortho, cut = 0.30, sort = TRUE)
## Factor Analysis using method =  pa
## Call: fa(r = quality_data_final_ortho, nfactors = 8, rotate = "varimax", 
##     fm = "pa")

## qd10    8 0.83                                    0.85 0.15 1.5
## qd19   17 0.83                                    0.84 0.16 1.5
## qd21   19 0.79                                    0.78 0.22 1.5
